headings.text: Mininet — Cargo workspace for the self-contained Rust core.
Founder Decision A1 now targets a custom Rust chain, not Cosmos/Go. Identity
and presence remain ledger-independent (SPEC-01 G8), so this workspace can
build the two-phone Bluetooth demo before the chain lands.
Deterministic codegen helps us toward SPEC-11's frozen "verified reproducible"
release requirement. (Full hermetic/reproducible builds are a later batch; this
is the cheap, non-surprising part.) panic strategy is left at the default so

title: Mininet — Cargo workspace for the self-contained Rust core.

headings.text: Before anything else: read the honest limits
Identity builds only on the crypto foundation. Keeping the dependency surface
this small is deliberate: this crate is security-critical and must stay easy to
review and reproduce (SPEC-11). It has NO network or chain dependency — SPEC-01
G8: identity is verifiable peer-to-peer, the chain is only an optional anchor.
Tests reconstruct expected keys directly from seeds.

title: Identity builds only on the crypto foundation. Keeping the dependency surface

headings.text: The channel composes Mininet's own vetted primitives (X25519 + HKDF-SHA256 +
ChaCha20-Poly1305) from mini-crypto. No direct third-party crypto here, and no
transport framework — the bearer is a dumb pipe.

title: Audited, widely-used primitives. The dependency surface is kept deliberately

headings.text: Frozen and security-critical invariants enforced as code
Only the local randomness primitive (ephemeral peer id generation) — no
external P2P framework dependency. The routing/gossip algorithms below are
Mininet-owned reimplementations of proven designs (Kademlia, epidemic
gossip broadcast), not a vendored third-party crate (D-0034 point 3).
examples/gossip_live_demo.rs only -- this crate's own library code stays
transport-agnostic (D-0042); the live demo is what actually puts gossip
frames on a real socket, via mini-bearer's TcpBearer.

title: Only the local randomness primitive (ephemeral peer id generation) — no

headings.text: Live demo: gossip over a real network (D-0042)

title: use mini_net::{fanout_peers, GossipRouter, PeerId, RoutingTable, BUCKET_SIZE};
use did_mini::{verify_delegation, Capabilities, Controller, Did, IndexedSig, Kel};

headings.text: The only dependency: raw signing/verification and hashing. This crate is
deliberately decoupled from mini-value's stealth/ring modules and from
did-mini's KEL layer -- a payer/payee "authority" here is just a public
key, so callers choose what kind of key it is (a stealth one-time key,
a did:mini device key, whatever fits) without this crate taking a
position on addressing or anonymity. Its only job is the settlement
state machine and reconciliation rules M1/M2/M3 require.

title: The only dependency: raw signing/verification and hashing. This crate is

title: use did_mini::{AvailabilityWindow, BaseDeviceRole, BatteryPolicy, Capabilities, Controller, Did};

headings.text: Same audited primitive-layer crate ed25519-dalek/x25519-dalek already
build on (D-0014's precedent) -- the raw Ristretto group only. The FROST
threshold-signature protocol built on top is Mininet-owned (D-0037).
"zeroize" is on by default upstream, but named explicitly here so this
crate's own zeroization guarantee (SigningNonces, issue #93) doesn't
depend on that staying true by accident.

headings.text: Scope rule (added D-0045 batch, per founder review)
D-0001 — On-device core language: Rust; single-language stack  ·  *Accepted*
D-0003 — Signature suite: Ed25519 default, behind a versioned tag  ·  *Provisional*
D-0004 — Content-address hash: BLAKE3 default, SHA-256 interop, SHA-1 forbidden  ·  *Accepted (freeze)*
D-0005 — Identity foundation: KERI-style autonomic identifiers  ·  *Accepted*
D-0006 — Reproducibility hygiene: commit `Cargo.lock`, pin the toolchain  ·  *Accepted*
D-0007 — did:mini wire profile: hand-rolled deterministic codec; BLAKE3 SAID-style SCID  ·  *Accepted*
D-0008 — Chain framework (A1): custom Rust chain on an adapted proven BFT  ·  *Accepted*
D-0009 — Networking core (A3): mesh + local Wi-Fi + optional relay; drop radio  ·  *Accepted*
D-0010 — Device delegation model (M2): delegated inception + mutual seal  ·  *Accepted*
D-0011 — Self-contained genesis and self-updating release registry  ·  *Accepted*
D-0012 — Bluetooth-only bootstrap is a mandatory launch path  ·  *Accepted*
D-0013 — did:mini wire hardening before first public release  ·  *Accepted*
D-0014 — Pack 1 crypto primitives for Bluetooth/local encrypted sessions  ·  *Accepted*
D-0015 — Bearer layer and anonymous encrypted channel (`mini-bearer`)  ·  *Accepted*
D-0016 — Presence attestations (`mini-presence`): range-bound, mutually signed  ·  *Accepted*
D-0017 — Reward accrual stub (`mini-reward`): slow, diversity-weighted vesting toward MINI  ·  *Accepted, corrected 2026-07-08*
D-0018 — Keystone harness (`mini-keystone`): the beta is the composed flow  ·  *Accepted*
D-0019 — UI beta stack: one Rust core, Flutter shell, WASM web, SPEC-09 objects  ·  *Proposed (founder veto open)*
D-0020 — Sovereignty-first UI & distribution stack  ·  *Accepted (founder directive)*
D-0028 — Merge governance & self-amending maintainer chain (`mini-forge::governance`)  ·  *Accepted*
D-0029 — External review integrated: hardening pass (Batches 7A/7B/7C-core)  ·  *Accepted*
D-0030 — Second review integrated: compile-readiness + honest quorum semantics  ·  *Accepted*
D-0031 — Third review integrated: truth-sync + root-carrier provenance + repo self-description  ·  *Accepted*
D-0032 — Parser strictness, artifact-only verifier rename, KEL cache caps, and repo-map restoration  ·  *Accepted*
D-0033 — Founder decisions batch: public walls, base device, seed-on-view, 2-approval floor, radio/Cosmos closed for good  ·  *Accepted*
D-0034 — Ranging (UWB), uniqueness graph, P2P networking (libp2p), and privacy-preserving value transfer: four founder decisions, phased  ·  *Accepted (founder directive, phased implementation)*

D-0035 — Whitepaper reconciliation: MINI unification, three-signal personhood, hybrid consensus, human-only crypto core — and one open contradiction  ·  *Accepted where noted; one item explicitly OPEN*
D-0036 — Founder override: AI-authored ring-signature/stealth-address prototype for `mini-value`, ahead of external audit  ·  *Accepted (explicit founder override of D-0035 point 5)*
D-0037 — Founder policy change: D-0035 point 5 generalized to "human review, AI authorship permitted"  ·  *Accepted (supersedes D-0036's narrow scope)*
D-0038 — Founder redesign: open-ended, multi-signal `HumanStatus` accumulator, replacing reliance on any single silver-bullet signal  ·  *Accepted*
D-0039 — `mini-spacetime`: Merkle/PDP storage proof implemented as the honest interim scheme  ·  *Accepted*
D-0040 — `mini-value`: Bulletproofs range proof implemented for confidential amounts  ·  *Accepted*
D-0041 — `mini-treasury`: FROST threshold-signature custody + live multi-device demo  ·  *Accepted*
D-0042 — `mini-bearer`: real TCP transport (`TcpBearer`) + a live multi-process gossip demo in `mini-net`  ·  *Accepted*
D-0043 — `docs/FOUNDER_DIRECTIVES.md` adopted as a canonical document  ·  *Accepted*
D-0044 — Master roadmap opened; first four audit issues closed; CI gained real dependency scanning and reproducibility checks  ·  *Accepted*
D-0045 — Canonical money finality during outages  ·  *Accepted*
D-0051 — Bounty & review system: money funds work, never a decision  ·  *Accepted*
D-0052 — Fork legitimacy: four checkable continuity criteria  ·  *Accepted*
D-0053 — Identity audit hardening: recovery, threshold policy, delegation-chain refusal  ·  *Accepted*
D-0054 — Personhood promotion requires a live seed-anchored signal  ·  *Accepted*
D-0055 — `mini-settlement`: offline transaction settlement, implementing M1/M2/M3  ·  *Accepted*
D-0056 — External Legitimacy Gates: naming what more code cannot close  ·  *Accepted*
D-0057 — README as a human trust gateway; audience-door docs  ·  *Accepted*
D-0058 — `mini-settlement`: rename `nonce` field to `sequence` (terminology correction, not a design change)  ·  *Accepted*
D-0059 — `mini-treasury`: zeroize FROST nonces on drop, gate trusted-dealer keygen behind an explicit acknowledgment  ·  *Accepted*

Directive 8 — The Human Is the Root of Trust

Directive 11 — The Weakest Device Matters Most

Directive 16 — Preserve the Voice/Value Wall at All Costs

headings.text: Group 1: Identity & devices (`did-mini`, self-certifying identifiers, delegation, pre-rotation)
Group 2: Personhood & Sybil resistance (`mini-uniqueness`, presence, vouching graph) — **the sharpest "maybe" in this review**
Group 4: Consensus & chain (`mini-chain`, validator sets, finality)
Group 7: AI's role (governance recommendations, code authorship, moderation)
What was checked and found correct
F1 (fixed) — Rotation silently rewrote the M-of-N policy to N-of-N
F2 (fixed) — `verify_delegation` accepted a delegated identity as a root
F3 (fixed) — Unscrubbed seed copies in pairwise-pseudonym derivation
F4 (open, launch-blocking for anything that needs revocation or recency) — No KEL freshness/duplicity layer
F5 (recorded, no action) — Century-scale KEL growth cap
F6 (recorded, no action) — Assumptions inherited from mini-crypto
Device-compromise summary (what a thief of one device gets)
The recovery model that now exists
Bugs found and fixed during this audit
Relay attack (attacker relays between two distant real devices) — ⚠ NOT fully defended (known, structural)
Time spoofing (forging `started_at_ms`/`finished_at_ms`) — ✅ defended for freshness, ⚠ inherent for absolute time
GPS spoofing (fake location commitments) — ✅ not trusted (by design)
BLE replay (replaying a captured legitimate exchange) — ✅ defended (multiple independent layers)
Device cloning (same device identity on two physical devices) — ✅ bounded to the same limit as all key custody
Finding F1 (fixed this batch) — Farm-saturation bypass of the honest graph
Long-term & sleeping Sybils — the age gate is a speed bump, not a wall
Nation-state Sybils — not defended, and honestly cannot be by this layer alone
2. `mini_objects::ObjectId` — is the id ever trusted instead of recomputed?
3. `mini_store::Store` — is content-addressing enforced on read, or only assumed?
2. Dependency tree: does `unsafe` appear, and is it expected?
3. Dependency-vulnerability scanning: a real finding from setting this up
